Google 


This  is  a  digital  copy  of  a  book  that  was  preserved  for  generations  on  library  shelves  before  it  was  carefully  scanned  by  Google  as  part  of  a  project 

to  make  the  world's  books  discoverable  online. 

It  has  survived  long  enough  for  the  copyright  to  expire  and  the  book  to  enter  the  public  domain.  A  public  domain  book  is  one  that  was  never  subject 

to  copyright  or  whose  legal  copyright  term  has  expired.  Whether  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  may  vary  country  to  country.  Public  domain  books 

are  our  gateways  to  the  past,  representing  a  wealth  of  history,  culture  and  knowledge  that's  often  difficult  to  discover. 

Marks,  notations  and  other  maiginalia  present  in  the  original  volume  will  appear  in  this  file  -  a  reminder  of  this  book's  long  journey  from  the 

publisher  to  a  library  and  finally  to  you. 

Usage  guidelines 

Google  is  proud  to  partner  with  libraries  to  digitize  public  domain  materials  and  make  them  widely  accessible.  Public  domain  books  belong  to  the 
public  and  we  are  merely  their  custodians.  Nevertheless,  this  work  is  expensive,  so  in  order  to  keep  providing  tliis  resource,  we  liave  taken  steps  to 
prevent  abuse  by  commercial  parties,  including  placing  technical  restrictions  on  automated  querying. 
We  also  ask  that  you: 

+  Make  non-commercial  use  of  the  files  We  designed  Google  Book  Search  for  use  by  individuals,  and  we  request  that  you  use  these  files  for 
personal,  non-commercial  purposes. 

+  Refrain  fivm  automated  querying  Do  not  send  automated  queries  of  any  sort  to  Google's  system:  If  you  are  conducting  research  on  machine 
translation,  optical  character  recognition  or  other  areas  where  access  to  a  large  amount  of  text  is  helpful,  please  contact  us.  We  encourage  the 
use  of  public  domain  materials  for  these  purposes  and  may  be  able  to  help. 

+  Maintain  attributionTht  GoogXt  "watermark"  you  see  on  each  file  is  essential  for  in  forming  people  about  this  project  and  helping  them  find 
additional  materials  through  Google  Book  Search.  Please  do  not  remove  it. 

+  Keep  it  legal  Whatever  your  use,  remember  that  you  are  responsible  for  ensuring  that  what  you  are  doing  is  legal.  Do  not  assume  that  just 
because  we  believe  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  the  United  States,  that  the  work  is  also  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  other 
countries.  Whether  a  book  is  still  in  copyright  varies  from  country  to  country,  and  we  can't  offer  guidance  on  whether  any  specific  use  of 
any  specific  book  is  allowed.  Please  do  not  assume  that  a  book's  appearance  in  Google  Book  Search  means  it  can  be  used  in  any  manner 
anywhere  in  the  world.  Copyright  infringement  liabili^  can  be  quite  severe. 

About  Google  Book  Search 

Google's  mission  is  to  organize  the  world's  information  and  to  make  it  universally  accessible  and  useful.   Google  Book  Search  helps  readers

■kill  in  title-p^B,  and  all  the  numerous  haters  of  books  are

caught  without  examination,  and  repeated  with  a  senseless 
triumph. 

To  this  triumph  Ignorance  is  perfectly  welcome  ■  she 
may,  if  she  chooses,  glory  tn  her  own  blindness  :  ftir  my 
pan,  I  shall  leave  her  lo  herself. 

It  is  clearly  for  the  interests  of  learning,  that  a  due  at- 
tention should  be  raised  to  all  its  curiosities.  The  value  of 
literary  history  has  been  duly  appreciated  by  all  men  of  cul- 
tivated minds ;  and  it  cannot  well  be  questioned,  that  the 
preeeat  zeal  for  the  possession  of  old  books  tends  to  en- 
courage and  advance  it.

N°  VI. 


Tf  Memoirs  of  Gourer  and  Chaucer. 

Of  ihe  two  great  Fathers  of  Enclish  Poetry,  Gower 
and  CnAiJCER,  it  would  be  difhcult  to  say  any  thing 
new.  Yet  a  work  which  undertakes  to  give  the  bioeraphy 
of  the  most  eminent  of  the  early  proHSfiors  of  this  high 
art,  woald  be  very  deficient  witKout  some  memorials  of 
those  celebrated  men.  It  has  struck  me  therefore  that 
under  these  circumstances,  when  so  much  h&B  lately 
been  written  about  them,  without  throwine  many  imw 
lights  on  their  history,  it  would  usefully  gratify  curiosity 
to  introduce  a  transcript  of  the  account  given  of  them  by 
the  celebrated  John  Leland,  one  of  the  first,  most 
learned,  and  most-accoaiplished  of  our  biographers  and 
critics. 

The  book,  in  which  these  memoirs  occur,  ihough  not 
-scarce,  is  now,  I  believe,  but  seldom  thotight  of,  and 
still  seldomer  consulted.     It  is  entitled: 

"  Comm^ntaru  de  ScrtpioribHS  Britannkis,  Juctore 
Joanne  Lelando  Londinense.  Ex  Autograpko  Lelan- 
dina  nunc  primus  edidit  Anlonius  Halt,  A.  M.  Coll. 
Reg.  Oxon.  Socius,  Oxonii  E  Theatro  Skeldoniano 
MDCCIX.'  Bvols.  8vo. 

The  opportunity  to  compare  the  very  words  of  this 
eloquent  old  scholar,  who  lived  within  a  century  and  an 
half  of  the  two  itkistnous  bards,  may  be  both  amusing 
and  useful.

Of  Godwin's  late  Life  of  Chaucer,  in  two  quarto, 
(or  four  octavo,)  vdliimes,  the  merits  and  defects  have 
already  been  accurately  sttiled.  It  ia'much  too  diffuse 
and  degressive;  and  contains  little  new  matter;  but  still 
it  is  both  entertaining  and  instructive.  The  severity  of 
witty  and  sportive  satire  has  nearly  driven  it  from  among 
the  living  records  of  the  press  to  sleep  in  dusty  corners 
beneath  tbe  mantle  of  oblivion.  The  ingenious  biogra- 
pher was  new  to  his  subject;  his  eyes  were  unaccus- 
tomed to  the  ways  of  hoar  antiquity ;  but  he  displays 
great  labour,  comprehensive  research,  much  occasional 
eloquence,  and  powers  of  criticism  of  the  higher  sort. 

Mr.  Todd's  Illustrations  are  distinguished  by  ^at 
accuracy  and  industry  of  investigation,  which  bave  justly 
obtained  him  a  high  place  among  our  modern  antiquaries 
and  commeatatqrs.

f  Imprinted  at  London  ly  lohn  Aa/deley,  dwellyng 
in  little  Britayne  streets  without  AldeTsgale.  JJ75. 
[410.  black  letter,  containing  nine  leaves.} 

Such  is  the  title  of  this  very  curious  tract,  which  is 
hriefly  mentioned  by  Warton  (Hislory  of  English  Poetry, 
IV.  75.)  as  one  of  the  first  books,  exhibiting  not  only 
the  tricks  but  the  language  of  thieves.  Mr.  W .  supposes 
it  to  have  been  taken  from  another  piece  of  ihe  same  de- 
scription, by  Thomas  Harman,  eniilled  A  Caueatfor 
Commen  Cvrsetors  vvlgarely  called  Uagahonts,  ice, 
1557,  but  this  cannot  be  the  case,  since  we  find  in  Her- 
bert's Ames,  {II.  885,)  that  an  edition  appeared  by  the 
same  printer,  so  early  as  1565.  The  second,  of  which 
we  here  propose  to  offer  some  account,  has  escaped  the 
obser\'atioa  of  these  diligent  bibliographers. 

The  source  from  which  the  information  contained  in 
the  tract  was  procured,  is  declared  in  the  following  lines 
at  the  hack  of  (he  title.

*'  The  Printer  to  the  Render. 
"  This  brotherhood  of  Uacabondes 
To  shew  that  there  be  such  in  deede: 
Both  juitices  and  men  of  landes, 
W7II  lestifye  it  if  it  nedc 
For  at  a  sesaionH  ai  they  ut. 
By  chaunce  a  Uacabond  was  got. 
Whopromysdc  if  they  would  him  spare. 
And  keepe  his  name  Inmi  knowledge  then: 
He  would  as  siraunge  a  thing  declare 
As  euer  they  knew  synce  tlicy  were  men. 
Bot  if  my  fellowea  do  know  (sayd  he,) 
Tliat  ihus  J  dyd,  they  would  kyll  me. 
They  graunting  hiro  this  his  request, 
Uc  dyd  dedare  as  here  is  read.

Tower,  1662,  and  has  less  pith  than  is  usual.     In  Ms 
"Single  Sacrifice,"  he  complains  that  he  was  not  suf- 
fered to  present  a  prayer  to  the  King,  Lords,  or  Parlia- 
ment: he  therefore  prefers  his  petition  to  the  throne  of 
grace!  This  was  meditated  and  composed  during  the 
Author's  close  confinement  in  the  Tower,  1663.     It  is 
followed  by  "  a  precantion  relating  to  the  lime  present, 
June  15,  1665."     To  this  succeeds  "  the  Author's  Epi- 
taph:"^  most  of  which  has  been  printed  in  the  Bib- 
liographer, I.  16.     And  the  tract  closes  with  "a 
petitionary  meditation  on  the  behalf  of  F.  S.  the  author's 
much  honoured  and  charitable  friend,  then  visited  by  a 
languishing  sickness."     This  well  sustains  the  character 
of  being  earnestly  supplicaiive,  and  meekly  pious. 

•  This  "  Warning-piece"  was  occasioned  by  a  sadden  fire 
nbich  happened  dunng  Ihe  niglit,  at  Lothbuiy,  in  the  city  of 
Xondon,  and  consumed  the  boute  of  a  citizen,  with  >l)  iti  tenantt.

"  It  was  seldom  my  Father's  practice,  especially  of  late

yet  I  verily  believe  it  was  designed  for  the  press,  by  those 
many  alterations*  he  had  made,  which  so  blur'd  the 
original  copy,  that  1  was  forced  lo  get  it  tianscribed,  be- 
fore it  was  St  for  mysell  or  suy  body  else  to  read.  And 
having  now  perused  it,  I  could  not  in  conscience  conceal

that  it  may  take  the  effect  himself  desires  in  his  Epistle

lick,  will  be  acccpiable  both  to  God  and  man:  which

^  A  Short  and  Plaine  Dialogve  concerning  the  vn- 
iaufulnes  of  playing  at  Cards  or  Tables,  or  any 
other  game  consislivg  in  chance  Offered  to  the  re- 
ligious consideration  of  all  such  as  make  conscience 
of  all  their  uaies.  i  Thessal.  v.  21.  Trie  all  tkivgs 
arid  keete  that  which  is  good.  Imprinted  at  London 
for  Richard  Boile.    Twelves.     Eight  leaves. 

Dedicated  by  James  Balmford  to  "  Matster  Lionel  Mnddi- 
son,  Maior,  the  Aldermen  his  brethren,  and  the  godly  Bur- 
gesses of  Newcastle  vpon  Tine;  and  what  he  had  theretofore 
propounded  to  ihein  in  tt-tiching  he  published  to  all  men  by

The  interlocutors  in  this  plain  Dialogue,  which  is 
"  brimful!  of  scripture,"  arc  the  Professor  and  Preacher. 

~"  pTofes.  The  wit  is  exercised  by  tables  and  cards,  there- 
fore ihey  be  no  lots. 

Preach.  Yctlotterie  is  vsed  by  casting  dice  and  by  sbu filing 
and  culling,  before  the  wit  is  exercised.  But  how  doth  ihi* 
follow  (  Because  cards  and  tables  bee  not  naked  lois,  consisting 
only  in  chance  (as  dice)  they  are  therefore  no  lots  at  all.  Al- 
though being  vscd  without  cogging,  or  packing,  they  consist 
principally  in  chance,  from  wlicnce  they  arc  to  receiue  deno- 
mination. In  which  respect  a  lot  is  called  in  Latin  Sots,  that 
is,  chance  or  hazard.  Ai:d  Li/ra  vpon  Preu.  l6,  sailh.  To 
vse  lots,  is,  by  a  variable  euent  of  some  sensible  thing,  to  de- 
termine some  doubtfull  or  vocertaine  matter,  as  to  draw  cuts 
or  to  cast  dice 

Profes.  Lots  are  secret,  and  the  whole  disposing  of  them  is 
of  God,  Pro.  16, 33,  but  it  is  otherwise  to  tables  or  cards. 

Preach.  Lots  are  cast  into  the  lap  by  mao,  and  that  openly, 
lest  conueiance  should  be  su-^pected  :  but  the  disposing  of  the 
chance  is  secret,  th.it  it  may  be  cha'ce  indeede,  and  wholly  of 
God,  who  directeth  all  things,  Prou.  l6,  13.  9,  33.  So  in 
tables,  man  by  faire  casting  dice  truly  made,  and  in  cardes  by 
shuffling  8c  cutting,  doth  openly  dispose  the  dice  and  cards  so, 
as  whereby  a  variable  euent  may  fdlow:  but  it  is  onely  and 
immediately  of  God  that  the  dice  bee  so  cast,  and  the  cards  so 
shuffled  and  cut,  as  that  this  or  that  game  followeth,  eicept 
there  be  cogging  and  packing.  So  that  in  faire  play  mans  wit 
is  not  exercised  in  disposing  of  the  chance,  but  in  making  the 
best  of  it  being  past  "  *  *

Our  poetical  historian  remarks,  that  the  numerous  as- 
trological tracts,  particularly  pieces  called  prognosttco' 
Hans,  published  in  the  reign  of  Queen  Elizabeth,  are  a 
proof  how  strangely  the  people  were  infatuated  with  that 
•ort  of  divination.  One  of  th*  most  remarkable  (con-

Leonard  Wright." 

Vice  and  folly  called  forth  the  warm  and  honest  in- 
dignation of  the  writer.  Rough,  pointed,  and  tempe- 
rately severe,  the  attack  was  general:  like  the  Fool  in  a 
crowd,  who,  fearful  a  knave  might  escape,  belaboured 
every  one.  The  usual  characters  of  usurers,  tawyers, 
courtierE,  priests,  matrons,  &c.  pass  more  conspicuously 
beneath  the  lash  of  ihe  satirist.

This  is  a  long  gossipping  note  upon  the  antiquity  and 

f)ropriety  (or  oibenvise)  of  wearing  beards  at  New  Col- 
ege,  Oxford;  in  which  the  ancient  Latin  version,  from 
Leviticus  chap,  xix.  27,  is  thus  reuderetl :  "  Ye  shall  not 
round  the  corners  of  your  heads,  neiiliershalt  thou  mar 
the  corners  of  thy  beard."  .  j\n  English  (ranslaiinn, 
printed  in  1^41,  thus  renders  it:  '"  Ye  shall  not  rounde 
thelockes  of  your  heades,  neyther  shalle  thou  marre  the 
tuftes  of  thy  bearde."

"  Beef  Hall. 

"  In  the  year  1352,  which  was  about  the  26ib  year  of  the 
fieign  of  K..  £dw,  in.  the  Unirereiiy  made  a  great  complaint 
about  the  vast  expetnes  (hey  had  been  at  in  repairing  Beef- 
Hall,  8ec.  This  hall  was  situated  in  Saint  Ebb's  paridb,  not 
lar  from  St.  Aidate's  church,  on  the  souih  side  of  the  lane  that 
to  thia  day  is  called  Beef-Lane.  John  Kowse,  the  Warwick 
antiquary,  mentions  it  in  the  JFi-agments  of  his  table  that  are 
preserved  by  Mr.  Leland  and  printed  by  me  at  the  end  of  the 
fourth  volume  of  Leland's  Kin.  It  is  said  there,  that  it  wa* 
near  St.  Aldate's  church,  and  that  it  was  for  civilians.  Mr. 
Wood  tells  us,  that  this  hall  was  so  called  from  the  sign  of  an 
OK  that  was  formerly  painted  either  in  one  of  tbe^windows,  or  ' 
else  over  ibe  gate;  though  others  doe  not  approve  of  this  deri- 
vatioo,  observing,  that  if  it  bad  been  called  so  from  the  sign  of 
an  ox,  the  true  name  must  have  been  Ox-Hall  ;  and  there- 
fore it  seenis  to  them,  that  in  old  lime,  before  it  was  purchased 
for  the  use  of  scholars,  a  club  met  liere,  and  (hat  they  had

itcholars}  lo  copfinn  which  opinion  they  altcdge  diverse  in- 
stance*.   There  are  some  rcmaiDS  of  this  hall  to  this  day."

*'  -Beadles. 

"  Tt  was  an  old  custom  for  the  beadles  to  dine  with  the 
Master  Inceptors  at  the  time  of  their  proceeding,  and  lo  be 
splendidly  enterlaiiied  upon  that  occasion.  JBut  whereas  at 
tuch  times  the  beadles  used  lo  ask  the  inceptors  to  gratify 
them  with  some  dishes  and  wine  and  ale  for  their  own  private 
families  (which  bronght  an  unnecessary  charge  upon  the  in- 
ceptors) a  statute  was  made  against  Buch  abuses,  and  such  an  ' 
extravagant  custom."  p.Sld- 

"  It  was,  rooreoi'cr,  decreed,  at  the  same  ttrac,  that  the 
superior  beadles  should,  besides  tbe  abovemenlioncd  allowance, 
given  ten  shillings  a  piece  to  each  of  the  inferior  beadles  every 
year  to  provide  themselves  with  shoes,  and  that  they  should 
every  year  at  the  laying  down  thdr  staves,  and  taking  ihem 
again,  be  bound  (o  oblige  themselves,  that  they  would  punctu- 
ally and  religiously  observe  this  order."  321.

publications  of  Hearne  which  relate  (o  Ri^gal  Bio- 
6RAPHT.  Annals,  Aiitiqnities,  and  Biography  in 
General,  will  form  the  subjects  of  the  remainder  of  (his 
Catalogue  Raisonni,  If  the  reader,  who  in  well  versed 
in  Hemfian  lore,  approve  of  the  inimite  but  desultory 
manner  in  which  the  foregoing  communications  are 
made,  the  compiler  of  this  Catalogue  will  never  think 
the  labour  it  has  cost  him  thrown  away.  Bibliography 
is  a  severe  study  ;  in  due  time  it  may  become  a  popular 
one.

The  muse  of  Rowlands  is  seldom  found  in  good  com- 
pany. Her  best  characters  are  generally  picked  up  by 
the  way  side  atnong  the  idle  and  vicious ;  sometimes  on 
benches  of  tippling  houses,  and  too  often  the  precincts 
of  Bridewell;  or  from  the  crowd  that  usually  waited  upoa 
a  delinquent  wearing  *'  Tybume-tiiTany."  Her  only 
interest  is  founded  upon  locality  of  description,  which 
may  bepresumed  a  faithful,  if  not  a  flatterin^copy  of  the 
limes.  An  address  from  '*the  Knave  of  Harts  to  his

Mr.  Malone  was  of  opinion  the  poem  had  been  taken 
from  the  French  nf  Boieteau,  rather  than  the  Italian  of 
Baadeoil;:^  by  the  restoration  of  the  above  titif,  it  will 
be  seen  the  translator  refers  only  to  the  Italian  author. 
However,  the  words  in  the  title  "  written  first  in  Italian 
by  Bandell,"  do  noi  even  imply  it  was  translated  from 
that  language,  and  it  may  be  noticed  in  support  of  the 
opinion  of  the  critic,  that  another  work  by  Broke,  printed 
at  nearly  the  same  time,  it  set  forth  as  "  translated  out  of 
French."  §  The  reference  to  Bandell"  might  be  for  the 
purpose  of  directing  the  attention  of  the  reader  to  the 
original  source.

epitaph  upon  him.

my  spirit  return'd  again.

This  work  is  dedicated  to  Sir  Francis  Bacon,  "  Hii 
IMaiestie  having  deigned  to  patronize  the  first  fruits  of 
,my  labors,  to  whom  (if  I  may  presume)  ought  I  rather 
(for  many  titles)  to  second  with  the  second,  then  to  your 
Honour?  who  (as  Siluerius  sailh  of  Cxsar)  hath 
honoured  learning  by  his  owne  labours,  so  all  the  learned 
labour  to  honour  you  with  their  labours?  Seeing  there- 
fore his  Maiestie  vouchsafed  to  accept  of  my  Treatise  of 
Angels,  deigne  likewise  (most  worthy  Pecre)  to  patro- 
nize this  of  Paradise,"  Sec.  Your  honour's  humbly 
devoted,  John  Salkeld. 

The  book  consists  of  sixty-six  chapters,  and  was  eri* 
dently  consulted  by  Milton :  I  have  remarked  several  coin- 
cideni  passages  between  it  and  that  poet's  "  magnum 
opus."  Upon  the  whole  it  is  a  very  curious  compilation, 
and  the  perusal  of  it  will  gratify  the  admirers  of  Paradise 
Lost.  It  does  not  appear  to  have  been  known  to  Mr. 
Todd..

This  book,  which  bears  no  date,  was  wiitten,  as  far  as 
1  can  judge  from  its  contents,  sometime  after  Henrjr 
VIII.  had  thrown  off  his  allegiance  to  the  papal  power, 
probably  about  the  year  1^545.  The  general  stile  of  the 
work  is  similar  to  the  "  Exhorialion"  addressed  to  the 
same  Monarch,  of  which  some  account  is  given  in  the 
Seventh  Volume  of  the  Certs.  Lit.  (page  36.)  The  pre- 
sent writer  does  itot  however  confine  his  censures  to  the 
Pope  alone,  but  extends  them  to  every  thing  relating  to 
popery  in  general,  and  bilteriy  inveighs,  in  most  of  hia 
pages,  against  such  of  the  Engli.^h  bishops  and  higher 
orders  of  the  clergy,  who,  in  any  degree,  adhered  to  the 
forms  and  regulanons  of  that  religion,  in  the  discharge 
of  their  ecclesiastical  duties.

Frequent  research  has  not  hitherto  enabled  me  to  fill 
up  the  chasm,  which  time,  pr  some  less  certain  adversary, 
has  made  in  my  copy  of  the  above  work.  From  the  un- 
productive result  of  my  inquiries,  I  am  almost  led  to 
doubt  the  existence  of  another  copy :  but  as  the  book  col- 
lector ought  always  to  qualify  his  assertion  with  respect 
to  a  particular  work  being  unique,  by  adding  to  such  an 
epithet  the  adverb  "  ffUherlo,"  I  must  cautiouSly  abstain 
from  exulting  in  the  fancied  possession  of  an  article, 
which  another  week's  experience  may  convince  me,  in- 
stead of  being  unique,  is  dual,  or  perhaps  plural.  Such 
things  have  been  '*  ere  now  i'  the  oliien  time,"  The 
conviction  even  of  the  undoubted  possession  of  a  BiRgle 
copy,  and  the  raptures  attendant  on  it,  ought  to  oc 
governed,  or  at  least  moderated,  by  the  consideration  of 
what  its  individuality  may  be  owing  to:  the  collector 
ought  to  reflect  on  the  circumstances  suggested  hj  an 
emmcDt  modern  critic,  "  that  a  fire  or  an  enterprising 
trunk-maker  that  should  take  off  nearly  the  whole  of  a 
'  worthless  work,  would  instantly  render  toe  small  remain- 
der invaluable."  f    Indeed,  the  effects  of  fire  on  booka

are  similar  lo  tho<!e  on  gnld ;  at  least  if  the  flame  docs 
not  render  the  suhstance  more  pure,  the  residuum  is  tiiore 
valuable.  I  cannot  imlulge  my  vanity  in  the  solitary 
con  tempi  at  ion  of  the  work  in  cjuestion,  without  reflect- 
ing that  its  rarity  probably  originates  in  its  insignifi- 
cance.  Nature  is  not  fond  of  perpetuating  a  race  of 
dwarft,  and  the  flower  which  possesses  neither  beauty  of 
colour,  nor  fragrance  of  smell,  has  no  hold  on  the  fos- 
tering protection  of  the  botanist>  who  leaves  it  to  die,  at 
it  lived,  unregarded.  * 

This  work  partakes  little  of  the  character  or  incidents 
of  the  genuine  romance :  giantsareunknown:  chivalry  is 
scarcely  noticed :  and  neiuier  Mahound  nor  Termagaunt 
are  invoked  by  any  unhappy  devil  of  a  Pagan :  we  read 
not  of  knights  or  ladies : 

"  No  Duriadana  waves  o'er  fabled  realui," 
and   coniequenlly   the    incidents,    thouch    improbable, 
-would  alsojustify  me  in  saying,  that  its  claim  to  the  title 
of  romance  is  more  than  apocryphal.     It  is  merely  a 
"  bislorie."' 

Perhaps,  however,  there  is  a  species  of  originality  in 
ibis  history,  which  may  entitle  it  to  some  little  conside- 
ration. I  confess,  1  am  at  a  loss  whether  to  view  it  as  a 
lurkine  satire  on  the  preceding  ages,  or  as  ^n  effusion  in- 
tendeu  merely  for  amusement:  to  enable  the  beau  of 
the  Elizabethan  age  lo  while  away  an  hour  before  he 
"  walked  in  Powles;"  or  lo  furnish  him  with  a  meagre 
outline  of  the  names  and  characters  of  the  preceding  half 
century.  In  either  case  the  author  was  cursed  with  a 
most  uninventive  brain.  I  have  been  compelled  to  give 
the  title  from  the  running  title  and  colophon,  as  the  copy 
DOW  before  me  is  deficient  in  title  page,  preface  (if  there 
was  any),  and  the  first  chapter.  I  b^in  therefore  with 
the  second,  in  which  the  three  tlarls  of  Weatmoreland, 
Somerset,  and  Durham,  came  to  visit  '*  Albion's  Queene" 
Katherine  in  a  prison,  in  which  she  was  confined  by  her 
husband  under  suspicion  of  a  criminal  intercourse  with 
the  Duke  of  Suffolk.    Before  I  proceed  in  giving  a  con-

Mr.  R.  C[aiholic]  P[riest],  or,  as  written  in  the  title, 
"  a  papisie."  This  sineular  work  displays  much  genius

oflanguage,  though  printed  at  Florence,  must  have  been 
>  the  production  of  an  bnglishmaii  well  acquainted  wiih 
his  native  tongue.  How  to  class  such  a  work  is  not 
easy  to  conjecture.  It  is  partly  allegorical,  founded  upoa 
ecripture,  and,  commencing  wiih  the  story  of  our  li rat 
parents,  relates  several  of  the  facts  attending  the  life  of

giiaeie  of  romance,  with  an  introduction  of  Sibellean 
oracles.  Pagan  ficiions,  and  portions  of  the  Roman  his- 
tory. It  appears  the  offspring  of  a  fervid  imagination, 
after  some  intercourse  with  the  world,  and  at  length  con- 
templating divine  subjects  in  the  cold  and  gloomy  seclu- 
sion of  a  cell.  •-

N"  VIII. 


%  Memoir  nf  Sir  Henry  ffolton. 

A  VERT  few  years  after  the  birth  of  Sir  Philip  Sydney 
at  Fenshurst,  Kent  produced  another  very  eminent, 
though  not  equally  illustrious,  man,  in  the  person  of  Sir 
Hbnby  Wotton,  who  was  born  at  the  seat  of  his  an- 
cestors of  Boughton-Malherb,  in  the  middle  of  ths 
County^  in  1568. 

The  Wottons  were  of  no  inconsiderable  distinction, 
bavine  possessed  this  lordship  for  nearly  three  centuries. 
Sir  Edward  Wotton,  the  poet's  grandfather,  was  Treasurer 
of  Calais,  and  pf  the  Privy  Council  to  K.  Hen.  Vtll. 
and  was  elder  brother  to  the  celebrated  Dr.  Nicholas 
Wotton  Dean  of  Canterbury  and  nine  timis  Ambas- 
sador to  Foreign  Princes  during  the  retgnsof  K.  Henry 
and  his  children.  Sir  Robert  Wujtton,  the  father  of  these, 
was  entrusted  by  K.  Edw.  TV.  with  the  Lieutenancy  of 
Gnisnes,  and  wjs  Knight  Porter  and  Comptroller  of 
Calais;  where  he  died,  and  lies  buried. 

Sir  Henry's  elder  brother,  who  was  afterwards  raised 
by  K.  James  I.  to  the  peerage  by  the  title  of  Lohd  Wot- 
ton, was  in  1585  sent  by  Q.  Elizabeth  Ambassador  to 
that  monarch  in  Scotland;  and  Dr.  Robertson  speak-  of 
him,  as  "a  man,  gay,  well-bred,  and  entertaining;  who 
excelled  in  all  the  exercises,  fur  which  James  had  a  pas- 
sion, amused  the  young  King  by  relating  the  adventures 
which  he  had  met  with,  and  the  observations  be  had 
VOL.  II.  p  made 


Do,i,,-,-,ih,.Googlc 


210 

made  (Jiirhig  a  long  residence  in  forel^  countries — bnt 
under  the  veil  of  ihcse  suptrficial  qualities,"  Dr.  Robert- 
sun  adds,  that  *'he  concealed  a  dangerous  and  intriguiag 
spirit.  He  soon  grew  into  Favour  with  James,  and  while 
he  was  >cetningly  attentive  only  to  pleasure  and  diver- 
sions, he  acquired  inflnence  over  the  public  councils,  lo 
a  degree,  which  was  indecent  for  strangers  to  possess."  • 

Isaac  Walton  observes,  that  from  this  account  of  his 
anceslnrs,  "  it  mty  appear  that  Sir  Henry  Wotton  was  a 
branch  of  such  a  Icinared,  as  left  a  stock  of  reputation  lo 
their  posterity  ;  such  reputation  as  might  kindle  a  gene- 
rous emulation  in  strangers,  and  preserve  a  noble  am- 
bition in  those  of  his  name  and  family,  to  perform  actions 
worthy  of  their  ancestors." 

Sir  Henry  was  the  only  son  of  the  second  marriage  of 
his  father  Thomas  Woiion,  Esq.  with  Eleanora  daughter 
of  Sir  William  Finch  of  l!lastwell,  in  Kent,  (ancestor  to 
Lord  Winchclsea)  and  widow  of  Robert  Morton,  of  the 
same  County,  Ksq. 

He  was  educated  at  Winchester  school,  and  thence  re- 
moved to  New  College,  Oxford ;  where  at  the  age  of 
twenty,  he  took  the  degree  of  A.  M.  Soon  after  this  he 
lost  his  father,  and  was  left  with  no  other  provision  than 
«  rent  charge  of  loo  marks  a  year. 

Having  remained  two  years  longer  at  Oxford,  he  re- 
solved to  exchange  his  books  for  the  study  of  the  \vorld, 
and  "  to  purchase  the  rich  treasure  of  foreign  know- 
ledge" by  travel.  He  stayed  abroad  nine  years;  of  which 
three  were  spent  iu  Germany,  and  six  in  Italy. 

He  now,  at  the  age  of  thirty,  returned  highly  accom- 
plished, and  was  taken  into  the  friendship  of  Robert,  Earl 
of  Essex,  who  made  him  one  of  his  Secretaries;  and 
whom  he  attended  both  in  two  voyages  to  sea  against. the 
Sjianiards,  and  to  Ireland.  On  the  Earl's  subsequent 
imprisonment  (too  soon  followed  by  his  death  on  the 
block)  he  fled  to  France;  whence  he  went  to  Italy. 
From  his  residence  at  Florence  be  had  an  opporiunitv  of 
performing  a  most  essential  service  to  King  James  just 
before  the  death  of  Queen  Elizabeth,  by  carrying  secret

In  this  employment  he  continuedalmojt  twenty  years; 
and  the  whole  time  preserved  and  increased  his  interest 
with  the  state;  well  knowing,  that  he  who  negotiates  a 
continued  business,  and  neglects  the  study  of  dispositions, 
usually  fails  in  his  proposed  ends.  "  But  in  this  Sir 
Henry  did  not  fail ;  for  by  a  fine  sorting  of  fit  presents, 
curious  and  not  cosily  entertainments,  always  sweetened

even  in  their  Italian  language,  he  first  got  and  still 
preserved  such  interest  in  the  state  of  Venice,  that  it  was 
observed,  (such  was  either  his  merit  or  his  modesty)  they 
never  denied  him  any  request."

Jasper  Scioppius,  a  restless  Ramanist,  printed  it  as 
the  principle  of  King  James's  religion,  and  excited  so 
strongly  the  Monarch's  resentment  for  the  supposed 
oversight  of  Sir  Henry,  that  it  was  not  appeased  without 
great  difficulty. 

Sir  Henry  returned  to  London  the  year  before  King 
James  died,  rather  impoverished  than  enriched;  ana 
was  promised,  as  a  reward  for  his  services,  the  reversion 
of  some  office,  which  might  be  turned  into  present 
money;  and  also  that  of  (he  Mastership  of  the  Rolls, 
then  held  by  Sir  Julius  Caesar,  who  did  not  die  till  1639* 
But  these  were  only  in  hope;  and  he  required  an  imme- 
diate support,  being  always  so  careless  of  money,  as 
though  our  Saviour's  words,  "  Care  not  for  to-morrow," 
were  to  be  literally  understood. 

""But  it  pleased  Providence,"  says  Walton,  "  that  in 
this  juncture  of  time  the  Provostsblp  of  his  Majesty's 
College  of  Eton  became  void  by  the  death  of  Mr.  Thomas 
Murray,  for  which  there  were  (as  the  place  deserved) 
many  earnest  and  powerful  suitors  to  the  King.  And  Sir 
Henry,  who  had  for  many  years,  like  Sisyphus,  rolled 
the  TCbtless  stone  of  a  state -employment,  knowing  ex- 
perimentally that  the  great  blessing  of  a  sweet  content 
was  not  to  be  found  in  the  mulliludes  of  men  or  business, 
and  that  a  college  was  the  fittest  place  to  nourish  holy 
thoughts,  and  to  afford  rest  both  to  his  body  and  his 
mind,  which  his  age  (being  now  almost  threescore  years) 
seemed  to  require,aid  iberefore  use  his  own  and  the  inie^ 
fest  of  all  his  friends  to  procure  that  place.  By  which 
means,  and  quitting  the  King  of  his  promised  rever- 
sionary ofGces,  and  a  piece  of  honest  policy  (which  I 
have  not  time  to  relate)  he  got  a  grant  of  it  from  his 
Majesty."  He  was  instiluiea  to  this  preferment  July 
26,  1624.

"Thus,"  concludes  Walton,  "the  circle  of  Sir  Henry 
Wotlon's  life,  that  circle  which  begun  at  Bougblon,* 
and  in  the  circumference  ihereof  did  first  touch  at  Win-

in  the  seventy-second  year  of  his  age,  at  Eton  College,

motto  on  a  plain  grave-stone  over  him.  Dying  worthy 
of  hi:«  name  and  family ;  worthy  of  the  love  and  favour 
of  so  many  princes  and  persons  of  eminent  wisdom  and 
learning;  worthy  of  the  trust  committed  unto  him  for 
the  service  of  his  Prince  and  country."

T.  VVarton,  in  hisK"'"  to  Milton's  Comiis,  has  in 
a  different  tone  from  his  usual  liberality  of  sentiment 
pronounced  Sir  Htnry  to  be  "  though  a  polite  scholar) 
on  the  whole  a  tnixeo  and  desullory  character." 

Tn  a  strict  sense  this  may  be  true,  but  surely  not  in 
the  way  of  censure.  He  mingled  the  character  of  an 
active  statesman  with  that  of  a  recluse  scholar;  and  he 
wandered  from  the  crooked  and  thorny  intrigues  of  diplo- 
macy into  the  flowery  paths  of  the  Muses,  But  is  it  not 
high  praise  to  have  been  thus  desultory  ? 

Sir  Henry  had  the  taste  to  be  among  the  first,  who 
were  delighted  with  Milton's  exquisite,  and  mostoriginal, 
Mask  of  Comus.  "  But,"  says  Warton,  "  Sir  Henry's 
conceptions  did  not  reach  to  the  higher  poetry  ofComus. 
Hewa*  rather  struck  with  the  pastoral  melliHuence  of  its 
lyrick  measures,  which  he  styles  a  certain  Dorlque 
fleliraof  in  Ikt  songs  and  odes,  than  with  its  graver  and 
more  ma)cstic  tones,  with  the  solemnity  and  variety  of 
its  peculiar  tone  of  original  invention.  This  drama  was 
not  to  he  generally  characterized  by  its  songs  and  odes: 
nor  do  1  know  that  softness  and  e«  eetness  are  particularly 
characierisiical  of  those  passages,  which  are  most  com- 
monly roueh  with  strong  and  crowded  images,  and  rich 
in  personification.  However,  the  song  to  Echo,  and 
the  initial  strains  of  Coinus'slnvitation,  are  much  in  the 
Style  which  Wotton  describes."  Ipsa  mollilies  are  the 
words  of  Wotton;  and  it  is  almost  hypercriticism  to  say 
that  It  does  not  jipply.  Exquisite  beauty  and  sweetness 
of  imagery,  if  not  softness  of  language,  distinguishes 
these,  inimitable  lyrics.  The  song  to  Echo  is  indeed 
harmony  itself.     And  how  bcautrful  is  this — 

"  By  the  rnaliy-f ringed  bank.

But  it  may  be  true,  that  Sir  Henry's  genius  was  not 
vuited  to  the  higher  conceptions  of  Milton.  Hiimind 
was  subtle  and  elegant  rather  than  sublime.  In  truth, 
the  habits  of  a  diplomatist,  and  of  a  great  poet,  are  alto- 
gether incompatible.  The  reserve  and  caution  of  the  one 
cannot  co-exist  with  the  wild  and  erratic  excursions  of 
the  other.  One  must  be  perpetually  watchful  of  tha  cor- 
rect movements  of  man  in  his  most  di^guitecl  and  arti- 
ficial state ;  the  other  loves  only  to  be  conversant  with 
the  best  parts  of  our  nature,  and  those  BOtnewhat  im- 
proved and  heightened  above  reality. 

But  for  moral  and  didactic  poetry  the  experience  of  a 
statesman  does  not  disqualify  him.  On  the  contrary,  it 
sometimes  gives  a  force  and  sincerity  to  his  sentiments, 
which  inspires  them  with  the  breathing  eloquence  of 
the  heart.     Thus  the  following  touching  stanzas :

Yet  there  are  many  very  curious  passages  m  Sir

bouse;  and  perhaps  Sir  Henry  found  Lord  Essex  too 
ductile  to  the  artifices  of  such  men  as  Henry  Cuffc, 
whose  character  he  draws  with  a  laconic  but  vigorous 
pen.  Sir  Henry  was  a  man  of  keen  sen.se,  now  matured 
oy  all  the  experience  of  some  years  conversation  abroad, 
sharpened  by  the  pressure  of  adversity.  The  Earl  bad  a 
flowing,  indiscreet,  and  ungovtrned  understanding,  which 
often,  1  doubt  not,  raised  the  disgust  of  the  other.  "To 
consider  Essex  and  Buckinffham,"  says  Wotton,  *'  in 
their  pure  naturals,  I  consider  the  Earl's  intellectual 
faculties  to  have  been  his  stronger  pari;  and  in  the 
Duke  his  practical."  Here  ihe  partiality  breaks  out; 
oiberwise  what  literary  man  would  set  practice  against 
intellect  ?  Buckingham  seems  to  have  been  a  mere  niaa 
of  bodily  attractions,  with  a  high  d?grce  of  mental  pre- 
sumption. To  compare  a  person  of  the  high  birih  and 
fortunes  of  Elssex,  who  attracted  by  his  splendid  qualifica-

EsscK  was  one,  whose  great  and  whose  weak  traits  were 
90  nearly  balanced  as  to  make  it  dotibtfol  whether  he  de- 
serves the  eminence  he  has  attained.  But  his  faults  were 
generally  allied  to  virtues;  and  if  our  judgment  approve 
not,  mir  affections  almost  always  gn  with  him. 

To  Buckingham  nothing  but  the  excessive  splendour 
nf  his  fortunes  could  have  blinded  his  cotemporaries. 
What  single  qualitv  had  he  of  a  man  who  meriie<l  to  be 
great?  Had  he  intellect,  knowledge,  experience,  prudence, 
or  even  private  morals?  He  was  childish,  selfish,  raih, 
insolent,  and  ungrateful.  And  what  praise  could  he 
claim  ?  That  he  had  a  handsome  person,  was  splendid  in 
bis  apparel,  elegant  in  his  manners,  and  profuse  in  his 
household  !  '*  He  had,"  says  Clarei>don,  "  an  adn>irahle 
affability  and  gentleness  lo  all  men." 

But  lo  return  to  the  character  of  Sir  Henry  Wotton. 
This  celebrated  man  seems  to  have  lived  in  a  perpeiaal 
■tmggle  between  his  curiosity  r^;arding  the  worid,  fo- 
mented by  hi»  ambition,  and  his  Tove  of  books  contem- 
plation and  quiet.  His  letters  lo  Sir  Edmund  Bacon, 
who  married  his  niece,  prove  his  strong  family  affections. 
His  heart  appears  to  have  been  moulded  with  a  high  de- 
gree of  moral  tenderness.  This  both  the  sentiments  at- 
tributed to  him  by  Walton,  and  the  cast  of  his  poems, 
fufliciently  evince. 

He  was  a  great  scholar;  and  more  especially  skilled 
in  languages;  and  on  these  acquisitions  Cowley's  megy 
pri  mi  pally  expatiates. 

When  a  mRn  who  is  qualiRed  to  shine  in  the  world  by 
his  intellectual  powers,  loves  solitude,  it  is  generally  for 
the  purpose  of  giving  a  wider  scope  to  his  ideas.     And

how  Tew  are  there  thus  endnwcd,  who  do  not  love  soli. 
rude?  Nor  though  it  be  the  theme  of  most  poets,  will

of  solitude. 

How  beautiful  is  that  expression  of  Sir  Henry  regard- 
ing his  visit  to  the  hall  of  his  nativity  tliat  he  there 
^'firund  a  restoration  of  his  slrength  by  the  connatural- 
ness  of  thai,  which  he  called  hh  genial  air."  Thirty 
years  have  passed  since  1  strayed  over  ibe  fields  where 
he  was  born  ;  but  1  have  not  yet  forgot  the  sciisaiions  I 
experienced  at  his  memory ;  or  the  fairy  light  which  was 
thrown  by  my  fancy  upon  the  scene : 

"  •— ■  inspiring  ibade 

By  godlike  poels  venerable  madel" 

When  he  came  back  to  the  hall  of  his  boyhood,  and 
felt  the  very  "  gales  redolent  of  joy  and  youth  that 
breathed  a  second  spring,"  when  he  reposed  beneath  the 
reverend  groves  that  had  shaded  his  ancestors,  he  not 
only  looked  backward  with  a  kind  of  painful  pleasure; 
but  probably  flattered  himself  that  there  his  name  should 
reniaiu  in  honoar  for  ages  to  come,  and  enshrine  bis  own 
reputarion  with  all  the  meliciwed  and  increasing  softness 
ftrhich  is  produced  by  time.  But  alas !  the  next  gcnera- 
rton  saw  the  name  end ;  and  hi  less  ihan  a  century  every 
acre  of  the  domain  was  passed  into  the  hands  of  strangers 
to  his  blood.  I  remember  it  was  a  day  of  contending 
storms  and  sunshine,  tuwanis  the  close  of  autumn,  when 
we  visited  it;  the  appearance  of  the  skies  cherished  the 
wild  workings  of  the  fancy ;  we  were  received  at  the  hos- 
pitable tabic  of  a  friend  now  gone  to  his  grave,  front 
whose  windows  we  looked  over  a  vast  expanse  of  country ; 
Over  "  hamlets  grey  and  dim -discovered  spires;"  while 
evening  was  dr.iwing  her  nianile  over  them.  Night 
eanie  on  with  tremendous  darkness;  and  many  a  long 
mile  had  we  to  traverse  over  a  wild  and -intricate  counlry' 
before  we  went  to  our  rest.  At  this  very  moment  I 
recollect  itwiih  the  kind  of  feeling  of  a  vivid  and  roman> 
tic  dream.  But  oh  I. how  few  expectations  of  that  en- 
thusiastic period  of  life  have  been  realised!  Shy,  re ' 
served.

"  He  that  wil  passe  into  a  Indies  eics.

"  When  that  a  cat  will  eate  no  milke.

phetic talent  which  I  have  discovered  in  it.  For  who  can 
read  the  following  passage  without  imagining  that  the 
writer  had  some  foretaste  of  ihe  bibliographical  produc- 
tions of  the  present  day,  thotigh  they  may  by  no  meaiid 
accord  with  hu  opinion  of  ihc  lightness  of  labour  be* 
stowed  upon  ihcm.

MicheJborae."  Then  fallow  the  argumeuti.  The  work

trt^. 

The  Golden  Grove  probably  formed  a  valuable  gos-' 
sipping  work  for  the  public,  as  the  author,  to  i^xlensive 
reading  and  quick  applicalion,  added  many  local  stories,

fiious  annotations  occasionally  seem  tinctured  with  a

count of  Atheists  he  describes  the  deatti  ol  Marlow,  with

Nor  will  he  flatter  great  ones  for  a  fee. 
Whose  worth  lies  in  their  wealth  j  for  such  as  be 
Are  able  to  discerne :  nor  will  be  fawne 
Vpon  his  patrons  (laying  truth  to  pawnc 
In  every  line)  unlcsse  in  him  he  finde 
An  honest  heart  grac'd  with  a  noble  minde : ' 
Not  like  a  temporizer,  who  will  hold 
Pace  with  his  vices,  onely  for  bis  gold, 
Who  scribles  much,  and  sbamctulT  praise  doth  gainc ; 
Thad  better  bin  undone  i  for  time  will  staine 
His  name  fi]T  ever:  most  men  do  detest 
All  verses  for  his  sake  i  but  yet  the  rest 
Are  ne're  tbe  worsej  for  such  this  time  I  borrow: 
I  have  digreu'd,  I'le  speak  of  blm  to  morrow. 

But  this  ingenious  poet  doth  rehearse 
Things  as  tbey  are,  or  should  be;  and  his  verse 
Not  Etuft  with  clouded  words,  or  conjuring  stratnes. 
Nor  thunder  claps,  which  might  distract  the  bruines 
or  honest  readers;  but  in  learmes  most  fit 
1"  cipresac  his  matter,  and  to  teach  them  wit.

He  doth  refine  conmts,  and  raise  them  ht^ier^ 
His  musique's  next  unto  tbe  angels  quire. 
Nor  doth  be  spin  it  thred-bare ;  he'll  begin 
New  fancies  as  he  goes;  the  itpring  within 
Buns  alwayes  fresh :  he  doth  not  trade  abroad 
With  borrowed  wtt,  nor  tread  (he  beaten  road. 

His  genius  works  wbrn  other  men  do  sleepe; 
His  aimes  are  hctvcniy,  and  his  judgements  deepe. 
He's  humble  still;  yon  cannot  make  him  know 
His  bwne  desert ;  he's  not  a  man  for  show ; 
He  doth  not  search  for  praise,  (he  loaths  all  such) 
He  thinks  he'?  simple,  though  he  knows  so  much. 

But  yet  to  shew  the  vUcnesse  of  that  brood 
That  doe  prefer  their  humours,  bate  all  good, 
Hee'll  baffle  sach  men,  and  he  scorns  the  oeit 
Of  venom-coupled  sots  :  silence  is  best 
To  answer  such  back-bilers :  he  will  slight 
Detracting  vassals  that  will  vomit  spigbt 
At  what  they  know  not,  and  will  look  asquint 
On  things  of  worth ;  what  ere  has  most  worth  in't 
They  slubber  most  with  gall ;  in  all  that's  evHl 
'ITiey'll  goe  as  far,  and  be  as  like  the  devil). 
As  all  their  wit  can  make  them :  oh  !  but  then 
They'll  fall  with  shame  before  tbe  poets  pen. 
Though  they  like  Xentes  whip  the  sea,  and  send 
A  challenge  to  the  hils;  yet  in  the  end 
'I'he  sea's  too  strong,  the  mountaines  are  too  high 
For  fooles  to  clamber  :  so  like  fooles  tbey  die. 

This  honest  poet  £nds  among  the  wise 
llis  due  respect :  for  they  hare  leam'd  to  prize 
?ersoDs,  and  things  of  worth :  and  still  his  bent 
Is  how  to  shame  the  vile,  and  give  content 
To  all  the  best.     Come,  take  him  as  you  find  him; 
Hee'll  think  of  yon,  though  you  doe  never  mind  him, 
Tume  all  his  verse  to  prose,  it  beares  the  sense 
And  lustre  of  a  poem  ;  and  from  thence 
True  worth  doth  spring.     The  poets  first  did  teadi 
Humanitie  to  men,  made  up  the  breach. 
That  rudenesse  made ;  all  usefull  arts  were  doath'd 
With  poets  wit :  why  should  it  then  be  loath'd  ?

[Moses  Browne,  who  rose  by  hU  own  merit  from  thehumfcle 
occupation  of  a  pen-cntter  to  the  station  of  a  respectable  divine 
of  tbechorch  of  En,;land,  was  bom  in  1704.  Early  in  liie  fae 
distinguished  himself  by  bU  poetical  talents;  and  when  only 
twenty  years  of  age  published  a  tragedy  and  a  farce,  called 
"  Polidus,"  and  "  All  bedevilled."  These  were  played  to- 
gether at  a  private  theatre  in  Sl  Alban's  street.  He  became 
afWwards  a  frequent  contributor  to  the  Gentleman's  Maga- 
zine, and,  as  £ir  as  concerned  the  poetical  part,  was,  for  a 
lone  time,  one  of  its  chief  supports. 

Sir  John  Hawkins  inhis  LifeofDr.  Johnson,  says.hewas 
X  candidate  for  the  fiiiypomids  prize,  alluded  to  in  ihe  Doc- 
tor's first  letter  to  Cave,  as  well  as  for  the  other  prizes  which 
Cave  proposed  for  Poems  on  particnlar  sut>jects;  in  all,  or 
most  of  which  compositiooi,  he  had  the  good  fortune  to  suc- 
ceed.*

^  The  EygktTragedie  of  Seneca,  entiluUd  Agamemnon. 
Translated  out  of  Latin  in  to  English,  hy  John 
Sludley,  Student  in  Trinltie  Colledge  {n  Cambridge, 
Imprinted  at  London  in  Flele  streat,  henoaik  the 
Conduit,  at  the  signe  of  S,  John  Euanselyst,  hy 
Thomas  ColwelL     Anno  Domini  M.  D.  LXrL 

Such  is  the  exact  title  of  a  volume,  which  is  declared 
by  Warton*  to  be  "exceedingly  scarce,  and  hardly  to 
be  found  in  the  choicest  libraries  of  those  who  collect 
our  poetry  in  black-  letter."  The  copy,  frobi  which  the 
present  extract  is  given,  was  left  to  the  Bodletan  hbrary 
Dy  the  learned  Selden.f  and  is  bound  with  six  other 
pieces  of  equal  rarity.  It  is  in  ^mall  octavo,  and  the  sig- 
natures extend  to  G. 

Of  Sludley  little  is  now  known.  In  his  dedication  to 
Sir  William  Cecil,  then  Chancellor  of  Cambridge,  he 


ihyGooglc 


373 

Infomis  US  that  he  was  educated  at  Westminster;*  the 
title  of  his  Agamemnon  shews  us  his  college,  and  Chet- 
wood,  on  whal  authority  I  knnw  not,  says  that  he  was 
killed  in  Flanders  in  1587  +  Wood  speaks  of  him  as  "a 
noted  poet  in  Queen  Elizabeth's  time,"  ^  and,  iirom  the 
numerous  commendatory  offerings  prefixed  to  his  perform- 
ance, it  seems  that  he  was  held  m  high  estimation  by  his 
contemporaries. 

The  present  volume,  alihniigh  mentioned  by  Ritson, 
does  not  appear  to  have  been  inspected  by  him,  since  it 
contains  several  additional  names  to  his  Biographia 
Poelica.  The  iirst  of  these  is  Thomas  Newce,  who  has 
prefixed  two  copies  of  verses,  one  in  Latin,  the  other  in 
English.  This  gentleman  was  a  Fellow  of  Pembroke 
a  ail  in  1362 ;  he  was  afterwards  Hector  of  Oxburgti,  Nor- 
folk; of  Becclea,  Weston  Market,  and  Vicar  of  Gayslcy, 
Suffolk;  and  Feb.  21,  1584—5,  became  Prebendary  of 
Ely  Cathedral.  He  died  at  Gaysley  on  the  Slh  of  No- 
vember, 1617,  where  he  was  buried,  and  had  an  epiuph 
in  verse,  given  to  his  memory,  from  which  we  learn  that 
he  bad  ^ve  sons  aod  seven  aaughters  by  his  wife  Anne, 
whodied  in  ifii3, 1  The  only  literary  production  now 
extant  of  Newce  is  his  translation  of  the  Octavia  of 
Seneca,  printed  in  Newton'b  Collection.  |] — W.  R.  has 
a)so  two  pieces,  one  Laiin,  and  one  English.  H.  C.  ii 
very  possibly  Henry  Campion  of  Emanuel  College,  who 
has  one  piece  in  Sorrowes  Joy,  1603.  Thomas  Delapeend 
is  only  known  from  his  unusnnDy  rare  little  volume.  The

Best  pleating  unto  those  who  do  not  care

To  fiode  ttiem  to  be  wicked  who  arc  witty. 
At  their  detractions  I  do  not  repine  j 
Their  poems  I  esteem  as  they  do  mine."

to  the  preservation  of  the  former ;  and  as  poetical  celebrity

niche  in  the  temple  of  "  aye-enduring  fame."  By  some

Theseus  appears  to  be  the  son  of  Mgcus,  King  of 
Atbens,  andTiiana,  the  daughter  of  Mcleager,  King  of 
Calidon.  Their  tarty  loves,  with  all  ihe  wooing  ihercdf 
I  must  pass  over.  The  negligent  gatliercr  has  omitted 
three  sheets  out  of  this  thin  tract.  For  some  reason  ihey 
take  ship  to  pass  from  Greece  to  Spain,  arc  attacked  and 
cont^uered  by  pirates,  who,  racher  unusual  in  the  pageii 
of  romance,  ungalUntly  set  the  lady  on  shore  in  France, 
and  bear  the  enamoured  prince  away,  who  thereby  be- 
comes chained  lo  the  o^r.  During  a  pilgrimage  of  four 
years  by  the  lady,  the  lover  obtains  his  release,  and  is  en- 
tcrtain«l  by  ana  esteemed  the  conlideaiial  servant  of  the 
Landsgrave  of  Hesscn,  who  resides  at  Fuesen.  The 
proffered  illicit  passion  of  Impio  being  refused,  she  re- 
vengefully determines  to  ruin  the  favourite  by  a  false  accti- 
sation  of^his  attempting  violence;  a  crime  so  heinous  in 
the  eye  of  the  law,  that  he  is  condemned  and  bound  to 
the  stake  lo  suffer  death.  Here,  as  usual,  commences  the 
disclosure  to  reward  innocence  and  bring  the  vicious  cha- 
racter to  ignominy.  A  Knight  of  Athens,  on  his  travels, 
discovers  the  Prince  at  the  stake,  and  proclaims  his  birth; 
Ihe  accuser  is  re-examined,  and  Impio  strangles  herself 
with  a  towel.  Titana,  at  this  period,  was  governess  to 
the  children  of  the  high  constable  of  Fuesen,  and  the 
finding  the  Prince  of  Greece  being  bruited  about;

swaded  it  was  her  Theseus:  well  she  could  not  be  quiet  in 
miad  till  she  saw  liim :  the  nest  day  she  made  an  excu^ie  to

The  topics  descanted  upon  are  numerous,  and  little 
incidental  relations,  with  apposite  gatherings  from  ancient 
writers,  crowd  everypagc.  Such  ebullitions  of  a  retentive 
memory  appear  to  have  been  adopted  by  romance  writers 
to  supply  tile  place  of  comhals  and  tournaments,  knights, 
vizards,  aad  enchantments,  when  they  rapidly  declined, 
from  want  of  novelty:  however,  the  composition,  though 
not  entirely  fabulous,  was  less  in  unison  with  general 
reading  and  manners  than  the  wonderful  tale  of  fiction, 
and  the  machinery  of  the  one  continues  to  be  admired

"  Might  mournfull  wailing  end  mjr  dales,

Where  golden  pleasure  beares  the  sway,

Euen  then,  alas,  poors  wretched  wight, 
my  gladsome  myrth  was  heauj  mone. 
My  new  sprung  rose  did  scarcely  bud, 

whet  straightway  blasting  all  was  gcHie. 
Yet  mauger  frowning  fortunes  spite, 

my  Bwetest  (I)  is  euer  one, 
Not  neare  by  byrth,  but  deare  by  lone, 
and  sure  more  faithfull  neuer  none : 
His  wilt  is  still  as  erst  it  was, 

no  froward  chaunce  can  chaunge  his  choise. 
In  lieu  whereof  ^me  sound  his  praise 
with  most  triumphant  ioy full  voice."

Haslbwuod.

It  is  the  best  of  any  of  that  kind  :

And  leads  his  march  with  her  refulgent  lance. 
Sings  he  of  love  ?  Cupid  about  him  lurks ; 
And  Venus  in  her  chariot  draws  his  works. 
'Whate'cr  his  subject  be,  he'll  make  it  fit 
To  live  hereafter  Emperor  of  Wit. 
-  He  is  the  Muse's  darling :  all  iho  Nine 
FluEbus  disclaim,  and  term  him  more  divine. 
1  he  wondrous  Tasso,  that  so  long  balhbonift 
The  sacred  laurel,  shall  remain  forlorn: 
Alonso  de  Ercilla,  that  in  strong 
And  mighty  lines  halh  Araucuna  sung: 
And  Sallust,  that  the  ancient  Hebreiv  stoty 
Hath  poeiiz'd, — submit  unio  j'our  glory: 
So  ilie  chief  swans  of  Tagus,  Ame,  and  Seine 
Must  yield  to  Thames,  and  veil  unto  your  strain. 

Hail,  generous  magazine  of  wit,  you  blight 
Planet  of  learning,  dissipate  the  night 
Of  dullness,  wherein  US  this  age  involves. 
And  from  our  ignorance  redeem  our  soub ! 

A  word  at  parting.  Sir:  I  could  not  choose 
Thus  to  congratidate  your  happy  Muse  : 
And,  though  I  vilify  your  worth,  my  zeal. 
And  so  in  mercy  think,  intended  well. 
The  world  will  find  your  lines  are  great  and  strong ; 
The  ni/iit  ultra  of  the  English  tongue."

The  reader  will  now  probably  think  that  specimens 
more  than  enough  have  been  given  of  bir  Aston's  verses. 
But  they  exhibit  the  character  of  his  life  as  well  as  of  his 
talents.  His  days  seemed  to  have  been  passed  between 
his  bottle,  his  books,  and  his  rhymes.  Perhaps  hie  ad- 
diction to  the  first  might  arise  from  the  cares  which 
overwhelmed  him;  for  being  a  Catholic  he  is  said  to 
have  suffered  much  for  his  religion,  and  for  the  cause  of 
K.  Charles  I.  who,  according  to  his  own  account,  re- 
warded him  with  a  Baronetage,  dated  about  the  Kth  of 
Jan.  1641,  which  was  however  afterwards  disputed  bf 
the  Officers  of  Arms,  his  patent  not  being  enrolled. 

His  mind  appears  to  have  been  much  cultivated  with 
learning;  and  it  is  clear  that  he  possessed  considerable 
talents :  but  he  eithibits  scarcely  any  marks  of  genius. 
He  is  never  pathetic,  sublime,  or  even  elegant;  but  it 
generally  characterized  by  a  kind  of  familiarity  which 
amounts  to  doggrel,  and  frequently  to  flatness  and  insi- 
pidity. Still  it  IS  impossible  to  read  notices  of  so  many 
of  his  cotemporaries,  whose  habits  of  life  are  recalled  to 
our  fancies,  without  feeling  a  subordinate  kind  of  plea- 
sure that  gives  these  domestic  rhymes  a  lively  attraction. 
Sir  Aston,  compared  with  most  of  his  associate  authors 
whom  be  commemorates,  displays  a  very  dim  light.

Yet  38  a  country-geDtleman  of  an  ancnnt  fvaily  ana 
good  patrimony,  he  calls  forth  no  amajl  tribute  of  re>

their  names  from  the  graves  in  which  their  bodies  are

The  beautiful  scenery  of  the  country,  the  leisure  and 
{lignity  uf  an  independent  life,  acting  on  a  polished  edu- 
cation, woaid  seem  in  theory  most  fitted  to  qualify  the 
human  intellect  for  the  utmost  refinement,  and  the  best 
efforts  of  genius.  But  alas  !  how  few  of  ihis  order  make 
use  of  the  great  opportunities  thus  bestowed  upon  them! 
We  see  ihem  disdainlul  of  books!  insensible  to  works  of 
reason  or  fancy  !  and  malicious  towards  any  amone 
ihemKelves,  who  by  any  chance  aspire  to  those  mental 
acquisitions  which  themselves  neglecti  I  am  not  sure 
that  the  manners  have  been  much  mended  since  it  has 
become  the  fashion  for  gentlemen  to  engage  with  such 
j  ardour  in  the  occupation  of  practical  farmmg.  Defend 
me  from  the  society  of  men,  all  "  whose  talk  is  of 
bullocks,"  and  of  sheep!  I  would  not  be  one  to  follow 
the  ploughman  to  measure  his  furrows,  and  watch  with 
a  surly  grudge  the  unnecessary  half  hour  he  may  spend 
at  his  nieaU!  To  men  of  ample  property  and  liberal  edu- 
cation Providence  has  surely  allotted  higher  duties,  and 
more  refined  amusements.  It  matters  little,  whether  I 
turn  bailiB*.  or  turn  groom.  If  I  desert  my  station  in  so- 
ciety, let  me  talce  the  consequence  of  my  owo  d^radatioD, 
and  be  fixed  there,  never  to  rise  again  !  Thrust  me 
among  clod -hoppers  and  stable-boys,  and  let  me  associate 
and  feed  with  them,  as  I  ought.  Ijut  do  not  let  me  in- 
trude lo  irritate  the  nerves,  or  damp  the  nicer  sense  of 
delight  of  ihose  uho  have  made  use  of  the  talent  God 
has  given  them  as  they  ought !  who  justify  the  station 
they  Gil  yet  more  by  the  superiority  of  their  employ- 
ments than  by  their  Birth  or  fortune! 

In  the  times  nfSir  Aston  Cokayne,  it  may  be  doubted^ 
if  the  minds  of  the  country  gentlemen  were  not  io  an 
higher  state  of  cultivation  than  they  are  now.  In  truth 
that  class  were  then  of  much  more  honourable  birth  than

After  oor  poet  had  sold  bis  lordship  of  Pocrfey  to 
Humphry  Jennings,  Esq.  and  his  lordship  of  Asbboame 
10  Sir  William  Boothby,  Bart,  he  retired  to  Derby,  where 
he  died  on  the  breaking  of  the  great  frost  Id  Feb.  1685, 
at  the  age  of  seventy-five. 

Wood  says  he  translated  into  English  ao  exceUent 
Italian  Romance,  called  Dianta,  1654. 

I  refer  for  a  character  of  his  Comedict  to  the  Bio- 
grapkia  Dramatica. 

His  Masque  at  Bretby  is  reprinted  in  the  third 
volume  of  the  Topographer;  where  also  may  be  found 
several  of  his  Epigrams. 

The  above  list  of  his  connections  may  not  be  uninte- 
resting lo  the  families  to  whom  they  belong. 

In  any  other  work  than  such  as  the  present,  Sir  Aston 
scarcely  deserves  the  notice  he  has  here  obtained.-  But  I 
believe  that  his  book  is  scarce,  and  it  contains'  many 
notices  of  ages  that  are  passed  away.  It  is  some  en- 
couragement lo  literature,  that  even  its  amateurs  can 
thus  have  their  fame  revived,  after  it  has  slept  for  nearly 
a  century  and  an  half.  I  love  in  my  fancy  to  assemble 
round  Sir  A&ton,  his  boon  companions,  and  to  listen  lo 
his  mingled  bursts  of  wit  and  raillery  and  literature  and 
verse.  IJut  how  inferior  was  he  in  genius  and  acquire- 
ntents,  in  that  pure  stream  of  natural  and  touching 
sentiment  which  is  one  of  the  first  attractions  of  unso- 
phisticated genius,  to  his  younger  cousin,  Charles  Cotton, 
a  man  equally  careless  in  his  fortunes,  but  of  a  refined 
and  exquisite  heart,  who  possessed  much  of  the  nicer 
and  more  tender  vein  of  Cowper,  which  might  have  dis- 
played itself  in  similar  compositions  had  the  age  and 
his'own  unpardonable  haste  allowed  it.  But  he  too  lived 
a  life  of  pecuniary  embarrassment,  productive  of  cares, 
'  which  chilled  and  froze  up  the  Pierian  fountain  of  his 
bosom  1 


Do,i,,-,-,ih,.Googlc 


4^3  '       ^ 

botom!  That  bosom  was  a  well-spring  of  genuine  poetry, 
which  scattered  iu  waters  without  economy  or  thought.*

The  Qualities  of  an  Angler, 
But  ere  I  further  go,  it  shall  behuve 
To  show  what  gilts  and  qualities  of  minde 
Belongs  to  him  that  doth  this  pastime  love; 
And  what  the  vertues  are  of  every  kinde. 
Without  the  which  it  were  in  vain  to  prove.

No  more  then  he  that  having  store  of  meaie.

And  makes  his  pleasure  to  bis  thoughts  agree.

The  first  is  faith,  not  wavering  and  unstable. 
But  such  as  had  that  holy  patriark  old,  Abraham, 
That  to  the  highest  was  so  acceptable, 
A^  his  increase  and  ofiipring  manifold 
Exceeded  far  the  stars  innumerable, 
bo  must  he  still  9  tirme  pcrswasion  hold. 

That  where  as  waters,  brooks  and  lakes  aboutid. 
There  store  offish  wiihaut  all  doubt  abound. 
For  nature  that  hath  made  no  empty  thing. 
But  all  her  workes  doth  well  and  wisely  frame. 
Hath  fil'd  each  brook,  each  river,  lake  and  spring.

More  than  by  art  or  skill  can  wdl  be  shown.

The  luramer.  May,  June,  and  July,  are  fittest  for  Angling.

FUb  comimonly  spawne  at  Michaltide,

Iliey  tliTust  up  little  brooks  to  ipawne,  the  trout  and  lalmcut 
will  have  lying  on  their  backs. 

All  the  summrr-tune,  great  fish  go  dowBwardi  to  deepes. 

Barre  netting,  and  night  booking,  where  yon  love  Angling. 

When  you  angle  at  ground,  your  line  must  be  no  longer 
than  your  rod. 

He  that  u  more  greedy  of  Ssb  then  sport,  let  hitn  have  three 
or  foure  angles  fitted  and  bailed,  and  layd  in  aeverall  pooles,

the  water  in  the  first  rising,  and  fish  in  the  streamc  at  ground 
with  a  red  worme,  you  may  load  your  scde  if  there  be  a  store.

wifiker,  or  such  like. 

]  use  a  pouch  or  parchment  with  many  severall  places  to 
put  my  hookes  and  lines  in. 

I  use  a  rod  of  ivo  parts,  tojoyne  in  the  raidst  when  I  come 
to  the  river,  with  two  pins,  and  a  little  hempe  waxed,  thu» 
the  pins  joyne  it,  the  hempe  fastens  it  firmely. 


A  whale-bone  made  round  no  bigger  than  a  wheat-straw  at 
the  top,  yields  well,  and  strikes  well. 

l^t  your  rod  be  wiiboot  knots;  tbcy  are  dangeroos  £>r 
breaking,  and  boughls  are  Iroulilesonie. 

Keep  your  rod,  neither  too  dry  nor  loo  moist,  lest  li:cj  grow 
brittle  or  rotten. 

When  you  angle  in  drought,  wet  your  rod,  it  will  not  bretk 
lo  soone. 

You  shall  hardly  gtt  a  rod  of  one  piece,  but  either  crookt, 
top  heavy,  or  unequall  growne. 

Enterprise  no  mans  ground  without  leave,  breake  no  nani 
bedge  lo  his  losse.

If  we  may  jgdg?  from  ihe  silcDce  of  our  predecessors 
in  the  field  of  biblio^rapliicat  research,  the  production,  of 
which  an  opportunity  now  occurs  of  giving  gome  ac- 
count, may  be  ranked  among  those,  which,  from  the 
devastation  of  casualty,  or  from  the  more  gradual  con- 
sumption of  lime,  is  arrived  at  that  pitch  of  rarity  which 
holds  out  the  most  irresistible  temptation  to  the  victims 
of  the  Bibliomania. 

To  gratify  the  curiosity  which  its  presumed  scarcity 
will  naturally  awaken  relative  to  its  contcuts,  I  have 
drawn  up  the  followitig  analysis,  so  far  as  the  mutilated 
fraemenl  before  me  would  allow,  (and  where  am  I  to 
look  for  a  more  perfect  copy?)  for  the  loan  of  which 
fragment  I  am  indebted  to  a  friend,  who  beguiles  the  1 
weariness  of  a  laborious  profession,  by  an  occasional  re-  J 
currence  ^o  the  blandishments  of  antiquated  literature.      ) 

Upon  first  glance  at  this  relic,  I  have  to  lament  the 
carelessness  ofBomc  former  owner  in  whose  custody  the 
liile-page  has  probably  met  with  destruction.  The 
remnant  consi«ts  of  sixty-four  unpaged  leaves,  one  being 
deficient  in  the  cej>tre,  and  a  considerable  number  at  the 
conclusion ;  the  whole  perhaps  constituting  about  three 
fourths  of  its  original  bulk.  The  size  duodecimo,  though 
with  the  usual  octavo  signatures. 

From  the  register  of  the  Stationers'  Company  (the 
only  place  wherein  I  can  find  the  above  mentioned)  it 
appears  that  Thomas  Colwell  had  license  in  the  year 
1565  to  print  *'  The  mosie  notable  history  of  the  lorde 
Maiidozze."  It  will  be  remembered  that  Culwell  was 
likewise  the  printer  of  De  la  Peend's  translation  of  the 
fable  of  Hermaphrodilus  and  Salmacis,  for  which  he  had 
license  in  the  same  year,  and  whi(.h  has  been  described

The  Princess  then  returns  a  courtly  answer,  and  Lord

At  this  period  of  the  poem  the  leaf  before  alluded  to

himself  to  be  in  fault,  he  soon  finds  pretext  for  following, 
and  having  overtaken 'her,  passionately  devotes  himself 
to  her  service.     After  much  free  avowal  on  both  sides, 
in  which  she  promises  to  give  satisfaction  fbr  her  fault 
on  her  return,   Mandozze   bends  home  with  feverish 
bosom,  leaving  the  lady  to  pursue  her  journey.

A  sermon  by  John  Alcock,  Bishop  of  Ely,  the  founder 
of  Jesus  College,  Camliridge.  The  text  is  "  Jhesus 
clamabat  (Quiliabet  aures  audiendi  audiat.)  Luc.  viiij." 
And  the  writer,  in  enforcing  the  necessity  of  attending 
r^ularly  to  divine, worship  and  taking  the  sacrament, 
has  introduced  what  would  now  appear  singular,  a  di- 
rection to  his  hearers  to  obtain  full  knowicdee  of  all  pro- 
clamations  as  registere<I  in  the  Court  o7  Chancery; 
citing  Magna  Charta  and  Bretton  in  support  of  bb  ad- 
monition.

The  reputed  author  of  ibie  rare  and  erudite  perform- 
ance was  the  learned  Dr.  John  Case.     His  biographer. 
Wood,  states  him  to  have  been  bom  at  Woo^tock  in

Oxfordshire:  edacatcd  iJhere  ta  Engjirii*  but  in  Latin 
parity  at  New  College,  and  pnriK  at  ClniM  CtMrcb,  ol 
bolh  which  [Jaccs  he  was  sHccevsively  t  cbonilcr.  In 
1564  he  was  elected  scholar  of  Saint  John's  Cotl^,  and 
was  afterwards  Fellow,  Master  of  Arts,  and  the  moat 
noted  dispiuant  and  philosopher  that  ever  before  set  foot 
in  thai  colleKC.  Being  inclined  to  the  Roman  Catholic 
persuasion  he  left  his  Fellowship,  and  on  30th  Dec. 
1574,  married  Elizabeth,  the  widow  of  one  Dobson, 
Keeper  of  the  Bocardo  prison.  He  afterwards  read  lec- 
tures on  kigic  and  philowphy  to  yming  meu  (mostly 
Catholics)  in  a  private  house  in  St.  Mary  Magdalen's 
parish,  and  there  bad  disputalions,  dedamattons  and 
exercises  as  in  colleges  and  halls,  and  many  eminent  men 
issued  thence.  He  published  ComnKntaries  on  Ari- 
stotle, and  was  much  respected  for  his  knowledge  in 
medicine,  of  which  he  was  made  (or  took  the  degree  of) 
Doctor,  1589,  and  the  same  year  obtained  the  Prebendary 
of  North  Aulion,  in  the  church  of  Samni.  From  his 
lectures  he  possessed  a  competent  estate,  the  greater 
portion  of  which  he  bequeathed  to  pious  uses.  He  died 
about  the  sixtieth  year  of  his  age,  on  the  a3d  January, 
1509,  and  was  interred  in  the  chapel  of  Saint  John's 
College,  where  a  monument  was  erected  to  his  memory.*

The  new  translation  by  Mr.  Soulhey,  in  4  vols.  121110. 
1803,  under  the  title  of  "  ilmadis  of  Gaul  by  Vasco 
JLoheira,"  has  diminished  the  inlerest  and  value  before 
attached  tn  this  work  of  Munday,  of  which  the  learned 
and  ingenious  translator  thus  speaks. 

"  I  should  have  abridged  from  the  English  Iranslaiion  had 
it  been  accurate;  that  the  character  of  the  language  might 
have  assimilated  better  with  the  work..  But  the  English  ver- 
sioQ,  which  bears  date  as  late  as  I6IS,  a  century  after  the  pub- 
lication of  the  book  in  Spain,  has  been  made  from  the  French; 
every  trait  of  mannen,  which  wero  foreign  to  D'Herberay,  or 
obsoltHe  in  his  time,  is  accordingly  omitted,  anU  all  the  foolish 
anachronisms  and  abominable  obscenities  of  the  Frenchman 
are  retained.  I  kept  my  eye  upon  it  as  I  proceeded,  for  tl)e 
purpose  of  preserving  its  language,  where  it  was  possible.  A 
modern  style  would  have  altered  the  character  of  the  book; 
as  far  as  was  in  my  power  I  have  avoided  that  fault,  not  bv 
intermixing  obsolete  wordi,  but  by  rendering  the  original  stmc- 
ture  of  sentences  as  literally  as  was  convenient,  aud  by  reject- 
ing modern  phraseology  and  forms  ofpeiiod.  It  cannot  be 
supposed  that  I  have  uniformly  succeeded  in  this  attempt;  the 
old  wine  must  t3>te  of  the  new  cask."  *

This  book  might  have  been  entitled  Volumen  AuUcut», 
or  a  Court-CaU-ndar  for  the  year  1637,  expanded  into 
adulatory  verse.  I'be  number  of  royal  and  noble  penoa> 
ages  here  enrolled,  amonnts  to  four  hundred  and  twenty ; 
all  of  whom,  in  proportion  lo  ihe  length  of  their  respec- 
tive names,  are  elaborately  lauded  by  Misiris  Fage,  in 
anagrammatized  motios  and  acroslical  essays:  the  let- 
ters of  each  name  forming  initial  letters  to  each  line,  and 
the  anagram  of  each  name  being  worked  upon  as  mate- 
rials for  the  metre.  A  few  specimens  may  be  admissible^ 
a»  the  volume  is  considered  cziremety  rare. 

Afi$r  a  prose  dedication  to  Charlts  the  First,  lo  hii 
Queen,  with  all  iheir  progeny  and  alliances;  after  a 
second,  to  James  Duke  ot  Lenox,  with  several  other 
peers,  &c.  the  authoress  proceeds  tu  exhibit  '*  Certaina 
Jtutes  for  the  true  discovery  of  perfect  Anagrammes:" 
which  she  thus  accommodates  to  all  the  absurd  purposes 
of  anagramniatical  torture. 

"  E  may  moit-wfaat  conclude  an  English  word. 

And  so  a  letter  at  a  need  afford. 

H  is  an  aspiratiOTi,  aud  no  letter; 

It  may  be  had  or  left,  which  we  think  better. 

I  may  Ik  I  ur  Y,  as  needc  require^ 

Q,  ever  after,  doth  a  u  desire; 

Two  V's  may  be  a  double  u;  and  then 

A  double  u  may  be  two  V's  again. 

X  may  divided  be ;  and  S  and  C 

May  by  that  letter  comprehended  be. 

Zi  a  double  S  may  comprrhrnd  :— 

And  lastly,  an  apoalrophe  may  eaw 

Sometimes  a  letter,  where  it  doth  not  please."

Warm  ihoughta,  free  spinis,  flattering 

Winters  self  into  a  spring? 

]n  som,  wouldst  see  •  a  man  thai  can 

Live  to  be  old,  and  still  a  man  ; 

Whose  latest  and  most  leaden  hours 

Fall  with  soft  wings,  stuck  with  soft  flowres : 

And  when  life's  sweet  fable  ends. 

His  soul  and  body  part  like  friends  : 

No  quarrels,  murmurcs,  no  delayj 

A  kiss,  a  sigh,  and  so  away  ? 

This  rare  one.  Reader,  wouldst  (hou  sec  ? 

Heark  hither,  and  thyself  be  he. 

B.  Cbashaw." 
F.  G.  Waldbom.

With  every  sentiment  of  respect  for  the  purity  of  the 
author's  intentions,  we  must  express  a  hope,  that  some 
of  his  representations,  in  this  latterwork,  are  considerably 
overcharged ;  oiherwise  the  superiority  that  is  frequently 
maintained  of  the  "  Golden  days  of  Elizabeth,"  over 
later  times,  must  be  deemed  partial  and  unfounded. 
The  vice  of  gambling,  upon  which  Whetstone  princi- 
pally treats,  appears  to  hsvc  been  carried  to  an  almost 
incredible  excess,  and  its  decrease  in  the  country,  (I  wisl) 
I  could  add  its  total  disappearance  from  it)  may  probably 
be  chiefly  ascribed  to  that  general  diffugion  of  literature 
and  science,  which  now  happily  prevails  amongst  us. 

Whetstone  first  inveighs  "  against  stage  playes;"  and 
as  the  genius  of  Shakespear  had  not  then  dawned  upon 
his  native  country,  his  censures  are  more  readily  tole- 
rated. We  must  also  suppose  that  his  censures  extend 
merely  to  immoral  and  IJccniious  performances,  as  the 
author  hioiself,  had  a  few  years  previously  appeared  in 
the  character  of  a  dramatic  writer.

T  see  no  reason  why  the  pages  of  tbe£iBLloaRAPHBR

generality  of  readers.  I  shall  therefore,  without  further

volume  bequeathed  by  Dr.  Rawlinson  of  St.  John's  Col- 
lege, to  the  University  of  Oxford,  and  now  preserved 
among  his  extensive  and  extremely  valuable  collection  in 
the  Bodleian.

When  our  author  flourished,  the  aniplilicatlon  of  the 
materials  which  produce  the  elegance  of  poetry,  ytt&  too 
commonly,  neglected.  The  playful  attributes  of  (incy 
and  imaginatioQ.were  not  lavishly  asitociated  with  the 
labours  of  the  Muse ;  but  while  the  delineation  of  an 
image  is  nearly  bare  of  every  omatrteilt  and  grace,  it  docs 
not  prevent  the  rOiigh  &hd  ittipressive  oulMtie  from  ex- 
hibiting the  hand  of  a  mastct.  Had  the  author  studiously 
remarked  the  effect  and  richness  of  the  Imagery  that 
adorn^  Sackville's ,  Induction,  iheH  newlv  printed,  he 
would  have  produced  a  more  picturesque,  if  not  an  elegant

chains  of  fashion,  but  that,  if  it  do  not  discover  a  power- 
ful originality,  does  not  prove  a  deficiency  of  jndgroent,

In  ihis  age  of  reprints,  perhaps  the  work  of  Bellenden 
might  not  be  unworthy  the  notice  of  some  enterprising 
editor.  Hector  Boyse,  &&  an  historian,  is  noted  chie6y 
for  his  fabulous  absurdity.  But  to  the  grealernumber 
of  those  who  cherish  an  attachment  to  bUtkletter 
volumes,  the  marvellous  legends,  and  wild  superstitions 
of  an  author  who  is  resolved  to  "  hold  each  strange  tale 
devotuty  true,"  must  frequently  prove  more  acceptable 
and  interesting,  than  a  concise  narrative  of  facts,  however 
elegantly  told.  To  such  hisiorians,  it  is  almost  needless 
to  observe,  wa  are  indebted  for  the  best  plays  of  Shak- 
speafi  and,  in  more  recent  times,  for  the  "  Lay  of  the 
last  Minstrel,  Marmion,  the  Lady  of  the  Lake,  and 
Don  Roderick." — While  the  aunotaUons  of  Scott  and 
Soulhcy  never  refer  to  such  writers  as  Hume  and  Ro- 
bertson, they  are  crowded  with  quotations  from  Froissart, 
Mathew  Paris,  Hector  Boyse,  and  the  Crontcon  Nurem- 
bergenses.     These  obsolete  authors  perhaps  contain  the

germs  of  mtny  future  poems,  which  will  be  ihe  d(f)ight

The  nightingale  that  sweetly  doth  cotnplaine. 

His  yrfng  once  lost,  or  for  Ms  louing  mate: 
To  fill  the  lieauens  and  fields  himsetfe  doth  irame,
